risk_of_bias.support_text: A randomisation chart was created before the study's start. Block randomisation in blocks of 4 by study co-ordinator.

The randomisation chart was secured on a password-protected document accessible only by the study co-ordinator and the Principal Investigator. Assignment to study condition was concealed from participants and study clinicians until the end of session 1.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: Blinding of participants and study personnel was not possible because of the nature of the interventions

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of outcome assessment (detection bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: The Independent Assessors for the study were doctoral-level clinical psychologists who were blinded to randomisation arm and to detailed study aims.

risk_of_bias.domain: Incomplete outcome data (attrition bias): All outcomes
risk_of_bias.support_text: 50% dropped out in the treatment group (QUIT) and 22% in the control (ETAU) - the difference between groups was significant. ITT used for analysis though.

characteristics.participants:
Number: 53
Average age: 50.5 years
Gender: 84.9% male
Sexuality: not reported
Race/ethnicity: 86.8% non‐Hispanic
Average cpd in past week: 14.83 in intervention and 15.39 in the control arm
Nicotine dependence: moderate nicotine dependence (FTND: mean (SD) = 5.9 (1.93))
characteristics.interventions:
Type of interventions: individual‐/group‐level interventions delivered directly to PLWH who smoke
All participants received:
60‐min psychoeducation session involving providing information about the negative health consequences of smoking in the context of HIV, basic strategies for quitting, and details on the transdermal NRT patch. Participants selected their quit date 6 weeks from the date of session 1.
NRT‐ transdermal nicotine patch for 8 weeks
Intervention:hybrid cognitive behavioural therapy targetting smoking cessation, anxiety, and depression simultaneously; the treatment includes 6 modules (treatment rationale, education, cognitive restructuring, exposure, problem‐solving, and relapse prevention) that are delivered across 9 x 60‐min sessions; 1 session every week. In each session, curricular content focuses on managing one’s health in the context of HIV. The intervention was delivered by doctoral‐level clinical psychology interns and postdoctoral fellows; individual, face‐to‐face.
Tailoring: in each session, curricular content focuses on managing one’s health in the context of HIV.
Control:enhanced standard smoking intervention comprising 4 post‐quit 10‐min sessions, stretched over 9 weeks, delivered by the study co‐ordinator or research associate. During these sessions, participants reported on their smoking during the previous week and were provided with NRT for the coming week. If participants were abstinent during the past week, they were praised, and strategies for managing nicotine withdrawal were discussed. If participants were not abstinent during the past week, they were encouraged to select another quit date, and preparation for quitting was briefly discussed. Delivered by study co‐ordinator or research associate; individual, face‐to‐face
characteristics.outcomes:
7 day ppa: no smoking, not even a puff, in the 7 days before any assessment using timeline follow‐back at 1, 2, 4 and 6 months
Validation: eCO (≤ 4 ppm)

Funding: "supported by grant R34 DA031038‐01 award to the first 3 authors by the National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA)"
Conflict of interest: "J.A.J.S. has received compensation from Microtransponder, Inc., and Aptinyx, Inc. for consulting. The remaining authors have no conflicts of interests to disclose."

sections.text:
Cigarette smoking contributes to over 480,000 deaths each year1 and is considered the leading cause of preventable death and disability in the United States. The prevalence of smoking among people living with HIV (PLWH) is 3 times than that observed in the general population.2,3 Smokers with HIV are also less likely to adhere to antiretroviral medication regimens and likely to experience virologic failure sooner than nonsmokers with HIV.4,5 Consequently, PLWH are at an increased risk of developing medical diseases and experiencing premature death as a consequence of both HIV and tobacco-related health complications.6,7 Given these observations, developing effective smoking cessation programs that meet the unique needs of PLWH is critical.8

One plausible strategy for tailoring smoking cessation treatments to PLWH is integrating established smoking cessation treatments with interventions that target anxiety and depressive pathology,9 which are reliable predictors of smoking cessation failure.10 Interventions that reduce anxiety and depression before a quit attempt enhance cessation success.11,12 Importantly, anxiety and depressive pathology is more common among smokers than among the general nonpsychiatric population,13,14 and these symptoms are particularly common among PLWH. Indeed, prevalence estimates of anxiety and depressive disorders in PLWH have been estimated to be at least 2 times greater than those of comparison samples.15

Despite the high rates of co-occurring tobacco use, anxiety/depressive problems, and related disorders among PLWH, there are no empirically supported interventions for this population.8 Surprisingly, there are virtually no smoking cessation protocols specifically catered to smokers with HIV, nor are there published studies that address the treatment of anxiety pathology among PLWH. Similarly, few treatment paradigms efficaciously target the needs of those managing the dual challenges of HIV and depressive symptoms.16 The current study aims to fill this gap by testing an innovative treatment developed to reduce both anxiety and depression symptoms in smokers diagnosed with HIV.

Capitalizing on emerging psychotherapy research that has demonstrated the efficacy and efficiency of transdiagnostic interventions,17 a 9-week transdiagnostic protocol9 that integrates evidenced-based standard smoking cessation treatment (ie, counseling plus pharmacotherapy)18 with evidence-based strategies for managing anxiety and depressive symptoms (ie, cognitive restructuring, exposure therapy, problem-solving)19 was developed. In the current trial, 53 adult smokers with HIV were randomly assigned to either the novel integrated intervention (QUIT; N = 26) or the brief enhanced standard smoking cessation protocol (ie, enhanced treatment as usual; ETAU; N = 27). The primary goal was to increase 7-day point-prevalence abstinence (PPA) at the end of treatment (EOT) and 6 months after the quit date. We hypothesized that higher rates of PPA would be observed in QUIT relative to ETAU. In addition, we predicted that QUIT participants would report significantly greater improvements in anxiety and depression symptom severity compared with ETAU participants.

## Participants

Participants (age range 30–64 years, M = 50.5, SD = 8.21) were recruited from community HIV primary care clinics in Boston, MA, and the Infectious Disease Unit at Massachusetts General Hospital from March 2013 to February 2015. Providers told potentially eligible patients about the study and offered them the study coordinator’s contact information. Those who contacted the study coordinator were screened for preliminary eligibility over the phone and eligible participants were invited to come for a baseline assessment. During baseline, and on receiving a complete description of the study, participants completed the written informed consent and were enrolled. After baseline, those still interested in participating were scheduled for Session 1 if they met the following eligibility criteria: (1) self-reported being HIV-positive, (2) being between age 18 and 65 years; (3) smoking at least 5 cigarettes per day; and (4) indicating at least a moderate level of motivation to quit smoking (5 of 10 on a Likert-type scale). No inclusion criteria were specified for the presence of minimum anxiety or depression symptoms to allow for inclusion of a maximally representative sample of smokers living with HIV. After completion of Session 1, participants were randomized. Figure 1 depicts the flow of enrollment, randomization, sessions, and follow-up assessments. Participants were compensated for each visit attended, receiving $30 for baseline; $15 for sessions 1–6; $25 for sessions 7–10 and the 1-, 2-, and 4-month follow-up assessments; and $50 for the 6-month follow-up assessment.

## Procedures

The Massachusetts General Hospital Institutional Review Board approved all study procedures, and all procedures were in accordance with the Helsinki Declaration of 1975, as revised in 2000. Safety was monitored by a data and safety monitoring board. All participants provided written informed consent before participation.

## Randomization

Block randomization in blocks of 4 was conducted by the study coordinator. Before the study’s start, a randomization chart was created, corresponding to each study identification number. The chart was secured on a password-protected document accessible only by the study coordinator and the Principal Investigator. Assignment to study condition was concealed from participants and study clinicians until the end of session 1.

## Interventions

Participants in both QUIT and ETAU received one 60-minute psychoeducation session before randomization (session 1). This session provided information about the negative health consequences of smoking in the context of HIV, basic strategies for quitting, and details on the transdermal nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) patch. During this session, participants selected their quit date 6 weeks from the date of session 1.

## QUIT

An intervention was designed to optimize smoking cessation outcomes in PLWH by integrating evidence-based behavioral and pharmacological strategies for smoking cessation (eg, cognitive behavioral therapy for smoking cessation, NRT)18 with empirically supported therapies for anxiety and depressive disorders (eg, cognitive restructuring, exposure therapy, and problem-solving).17,19 The rationale for the intervention and its components have been described in detail in a previous report.9 In short, QUIT is a hybrid treatment that targets smoking cessation, anxiety, and depression simultaneously; the treatment includes 6 modules (treatment rationale, education, cognitive restructuring, exposure, problem-solving, and relapse prevention) that are delivered across nine 60-minute sessions. In each session, curricular content focuses on managing one’s health in the context of HIV. Quit Day was set for week 6 of the intervention, at which time participants were provided with NRT.9 Doctoral-level clinical psychology interns and postdoctoral fellows served as therapists. They received formalized training and ongoing clinical supervision from the first author.

## ETAU

Corresponding to weeks 7–10 of the QUIT intervention, control condition participants received 4 post-quit 10-minute sessions delivered by the study coordinator or research associate. During these sessions, participants reported on their smoking during the previous week and were provided with NRT for the coming week. If participants were abstinent during the past week, they were praised and strategies for managing nicotine withdrawal were discussed. If participants were not abstinent during the past week, they were encouraged to select another quit date, and preparation for quitting was briefly discussed.

## Assessment and Measures

The Independent Assessors for the study were doctoral-level clinical psychologists who were blinded to randomization arm and to detailed study aims. The Independent Assessors were neither part of clinical supervision nor study operations, and they completed follow-up abstinence assessments as well as assessments of anxiety and depression symptom severity. Baseline assessments were conducted before randomization. Self-report measures were completed on a tablet using electronic data capture.

## Screening and Sample Characteristics

Demographic data were collected by self-report. During baseline, the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-IV-TR Axis I Disorders, Research Version, Patient Edition (SCID-I/P)20 was administered to assess diagnostic exclusions and lifetime prevalence of Axis I disorders. The smoking history questionnaire,21 a single-item Likert-type 0–10 scale that assesses a participant’s motivation to quit smoking, and the Fagerström Test for Nicotine Dependence22 were also administered.

## Primary Outcome

Self-reported smoking status was assessed weekly from baseline through week 10 (EOT) for those in QUIT; at baseline and sessions 1 and 7–10 for those in ETAU; and at 1, 2, 4, and 6 months after quit date for all participants using the timeline follow-back procedure.23 PPA was defined as no smoking, not even a puff, in the 7 days before any assessment.24 Expired carbon monoxide (CO) was used to verify self-reported PPA at each assessment. Abstinence was defined as smoking 0 cigarettes in the previous 7 days and CO levels ≤4 ppm, as per research suggesting that this cutoff provides most accurate results for validating 24-cessation.25 If either timeline follow-back or CO was missing for an assessment, PPA was treated as missing.11,26 Saliva assays were collected and frozen to conduct cotinine assay for additional verification of abstinence; however, due to a storage error after a freezer malfunction, the saliva samples were unusable.

## Secondary Outcomes

Interviewer-rated measures of anxiety and depression symptom severity included the Structured Interview Guide for the Hamilton Anxiety (SIGH-A) Scale 27 and the Montgomery–Asberg Rating Scale for Depression (MADRS).28 These measures were administered at baseline and 6-month follow-up. Self-reported measures of anxiety and depression symptom severity included the State–Trait Anxiety Inventory-state anxiety subscale (STAI-S)29 and the Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (CES-D).30 These self-report scales were administered at baseline; treatment sessions 6, 8, and 10; and 1, 2, 4, and 6-month follow-ups.

## Data Analyses

### Primary Outcome

Abstinence data (7-day PPA) were analyzed using generalized linear mixed models (GLMM), which are multilevel models that use a logistic linking function. GLMM is an intent-to-treat analysis that includes all subjects and does not require imputation of missing data. GLMM computes its results from the obtained data and treats missing data as missing. It is the recommended approach for analyzing longitudinal smoking cessation trials.31 Multilevel models have been shown to make unbiased estimates of effects in samples as small as 30 participants.32

The pattern of change in PPA over time was complex (Fig. 2A) and did not follow any functional growth curve model or piecewise model. Based on these data, PPA was freely estimated at each assessment, which allowed it to vary without following a specific growth curve [as in an analysis of variance (ANOVA)]. Including all assessments of PPA in this model created an intent-to-treat analysis, which allowed a priori contrasts that compared PPA in QUIT vs. ETAU at EOT and at the final 6-month follow-up.

## Data Analyses

### Secondary Outcomes

Because there were 4 measures of anxiety and depressive symptom severity (SIGH-A, STAI-S, MADRS, and CES-D), these outcomes were analyzed using multivariate multilevel models (MMLM)33 to avoid inflation of type 1 error. MMLM does not require that all outcomes be measured on the same assessment schedule; this approach includes all assessments for an individual in which at least one outcome measure was obtained, thereby increasing generalizability and power. To be consistent with the GLMM model for abstinence described above, the longitudinal model used to analyze the psychiatric data was the MMLM equivalent of an ANOVA. In accordance with published recommendations,33 each psychological measure was z-scored to put all outcomes on the same scale. To increase power and to fully equalize groups on baseline levels of the outcome measures, baseline scores on the psychological variables were used as covariates in the MMLM analyses. Furthermore, per protocol, sex, education, and baseline nicotine dependence were also included as covariates. Sex was not significant in any of the analyses; therefore, it was deleted from the final models. MMLM analyses were followed by individual univariate analyses of the individual psychiatric outcomes. Effect sizes were estimated using the t to d conversion. All analyses were performed using SPSS 23.0.

## Sample Characteristics

The 53 participants (NQUIT = 26, NETAU = 27) were recruited between March 2013 and February 2015, and follow-up data collection was completed in October 2015. The accrual phase of the study continued 4 months past the planned study end date to allow for continued enrollment. As such, study accrual ended after the randomization of the 53rd participant. See Table 1 for sample characteristics. Participants were primarily male (84.9%) with at least some college education (65.4%). On average, participants reported moderate nicotine dependence at baseline (Fagerström Test for Nicotine Dependence M = 5.9, SD = 1.93). Six participants met diagnostic criteria for panic disorder, 4 for social phobia, 1 for obsessive compulsive disorder, and 5 for posttraumatic stress disorder. Five participants met diagnostic criteria for current major depressive disorder, and 22 met criteria for past major depressive disorder. Seven participants met diagnostic criteria for alcohol dependence, and 7 met for substance use dependence other than nicotine. χ2 analyses and t tests indicated no significant baseline differences between treatment groups on these or any of the other study variables (including baseline number of cigarettes smoked).

## Attrition

Eleven participants (20.8%) dropped out of the study before EOT. Most of these participants dropped before quit week (9 of 11). An additional 8 participants (15%) dropped during follow-up. Treatment dropout rates did not significantly differ by condition (7 of 26 in QUIT, 4 of 27 in ETAU, P = 0.33), nor did follow-up dropouts (6 in QUIT, 2 in ETAU, P = 0.14). However, total dropouts were significantly higher in QUIT than in ETAU (13 vs. 6, P = 0.047). Dropouts did not differ from nondropouts on any of the baseline demographics or study variables (ps = 0.18–0.91). These results are consistent with the data being “missing at random,” an assumption of GLMM and MMLM, although one cannot prove that data are actually missing at random.

## Primary Outcome

Abstinence increased from quit week through EOT for participants in QUIT, but not for those in ETAU (Fig. 2A). Hence, abstinence was significantly higher at EOT for QUIT than for ETAU [MQUIT = 59%, METAU = 9%; b = 5.60, 95% confidence interval (CI): (2.64 to 8.56), t(332) = 3.72, P < 0.001, d = 1.03]. Although abstinence decreased during the follow-up period in QUIT, abstinence was still significantly higher in QUIT than in ETAU at 6-month follow-up [MQUIT = 46%, MTAU = 5%; b = 7.69, 95% CI: (4.60 to 10.78), t(332) = 4.90, P < 0.001, d = 1.36].

Because one cannot prove that data are missing at random, one can test “missing not at random” models to examine whether results differ between those with missing data and those without missing data.34,35 To determine if dropouts or missing data impacted our results, a pattern mixture model34,35 was performed, which examined dropout status and missing data status as potential moderators of the treatment effects. These analyses (one for dropout and one for missing data status) showed no moderating effect of dropouts or missing data on abstinence (ps. 0.52). In sum, our pattern of results was not significantly altered by the missing data.

However, dropouts did show higher overall abstinence rates than nondropouts across treatments [b = 0.89, 95% CI: (0.14 to 1.63), t(331) = 2.34, P = 0.02, d = 65].

Given the differential dropout rates between treatments, post hoc sensitivity analyses were performed to further examine whether dropout impacted the results. These sensitivity analyses were designed to determine whether treatment effects were robust to different assumptions about missing data. Missing data were recoded (imputed) in 2 additional ways: (1) using last observation carried forward (LOCF; Fig. 2B) and (2) coding all missing data as “smoking.”26 The GLMM ANOVA model indicated that, for both missing data protocols, abstinence was still higher in QUIT than in ETAU at EOT [MQUIT = 38%, METAU = 7%; b = 2.06, 95% CI: (0.58 to 3.54), t(459) = 2.73, P = 0.007, d = 0.76 for both LOCF and missing = smoking] and at the 6-month follow-up [LOCF: MQUIT = 35%, METAU = 4%; b = 2.62, 95% CI: (0.68 to 4.56), t(459) = 2.65, P = 0.008, d = 0.73; and missing = smoking: MQUIT = 23%, METAU = 4%; b = 2.05, 95% CI: (0.08 to 4.03), t(459) = 2.04, P = 0.042, d = 0.57].

Because other reports have used a less stringent cutoff for validating 24-hour cessation, we examined a redefined abstinence outcome as 0 cigarettes smoked in the previous 7 days and CO levels #8 ppm. Results for using this alternative abstinence variable were identical to the results for the primary abstinence variable reported above in terms of significance, although abstinence rates were slightly higher in both conditions using this more liberal value.

## Secondary Outcomes

MMLM analyses indicated that at EOT, the multivariate outcome of anxiety and depressive symptom severity was lower in QUIT than in ETAU [b = 0.46, 95% CI: (0.09 to 0.84), t(252) = 2.41, P = 0.016, d = 0.67] (Fig. 3). Both self-rated outcomes that were assessed at EOT (STAI-S and CES-D) were lower in QUIT than ETAU (MADRS and SIGH-A were assessed at baseline and 6-month follow-up) [STAI-S: b = 0.54, 95% CI: (0.11 to 0.97), t(257) = 2.48, P = 0.014, d = 0.69; and CES-D: b = 0.56, 95% CI: (0.05 to 1.02), t(216) = 2.15, P = 0.033, d = 0.60; Figure 3]. MMLM analyses also showed that anxiety and depressive symptom severity was significantly lower in QUIT than in ETAU at the 6-month follow-up [b = 0.37, 95% CI: (0.05 to 0.69), t(95) = 2.30, P = 0.024, d = 0.64]. The treatment condition differences reached significance only for STAI-S and CES-D, perhaps due to limited power for the SIGH-A and MADRS because these 2 inventories were given only at baseline and 6-month follow-up [b = 0.41, 95% CI: (0.002 to 0.82), t(246) = 1.98, P = 0.049, d = 0.55; and b = 0.58, 95% CI: (0.10 to 1.06), t(199) = 2.40, P = 0.017, d = 0.67, respectively].

In this randomized controlled trial (RCT), an intervention that combined evidence-based smoking cessation treatment with evidence-based intervention strategies for anxiety and depression led to greater rates of smoking abstinence at the EOT and at 6-month follow-up compared with a standard smoking cessation protocol among smokers with HIV. Across different analyses that accounted for attrition and missing data, effect sizes for the between-group differences were large. These large effect sizes underscore the promise of the transdiagnostic intervention to meaningfully improve smoking cessation outcomes in this population, which is at high risk of cessation failure and subsequent smoking-related health issues. However, we should note that treatment condition differences were smaller in the LOCF and missing = smoking sensitivity analyses. These differences are not surprising because (1) LOCF tends to underestimate treatment condition differences because it carries forward results for participants who only receive partial treatment,36 and (2) coding missing data as smoking for participants in both treatment conditions is effectively imputing no treatment effect for participants at those missing assessments, thereby shrinking the average overall treatment effect. Thus, our primary analysis, GLMM, is the recommended analysis for smoking and psychological outcomes.31,36

Consistent with the model driving the development of the integrated intervention,9,10 the QUIT intervention also led to significant reductions in anxiety and depression symptom severity. Relative to those randomized to receive ETAU, participants assigned to the integrated intervention evidenced significantly greater reductions on measures tapping anxiety and depression-symptom severity overall. Interestingly though, the between-group differences only reached significance on self-reported measures. This discrepancy between self-reported and interviewer-rated measures has been observed in previous studies.37,38 In the current study, it seems plausible that the statistical power was limited to detect significant differences on the interviewer-rated measures because the assessment schedule included fewer administrations of interviewer-rated measures than self-reported measures.

## Limitations

Several limitations warrant comment. First, although the total dropout rate fell within the observed range reported across other psychosocial interventions for PLWH,39 attrition was relatively high [19 (35.8%)]. To address this limitation, statistical tests were conducted that provided evidence for the robustness of the smoking abstinence findings despite missing data. Nevertheless, the smaller sample size and decreased power may, in part, have contributed to the lack of statistical differences between groups on interviewer-administered measures of anxiety and depression symptom severity. Second, to enhance compliance to the assessment protocol, we remunerated participants but, as a result, may have limited the generalizability of the findings to clinical setting. Third, the male-to-female ratio was representative of the ratio of HIV rates observed in the general population40 and therefore, increases the generalizability of the present findings. However, as a natural by-product of HIV disparities across sex, few women were enrolled in the study, which did not permit the testing of sex differences. The extent to which men and women respond similarly to treatment remains unknown. Fourth, to equate the 2 groups for protocol length, we spaced the sessions in the ETAU condition. This may have inadvertently reduced motivation to quit among participants assigned to ETAU. Fifth, we should note that it took several years to recruit this modest size sample, which possibly limits the external validity of the findings. Finally, this is the first study to test the efficacy of an integrated treatment for smoking cessation and anxiety/depression among smokers with HIV. Considering the novelty of such work, we focused on between-person effects. There is a growing interest in the evaluation of psychosocial treatments using a process–outcome framework.41 Although this study provides the necessary foundation to support future process–outcome research on integrated treatment options for smokers with HIV, it does not address these important research questions.

Among PLWH, an integrated treatment that combined evidence-based standard smoking cessation with evidence-based intervention strategies for anxiety and depression was more efficacious in decreasing both smoking and anxiety/depressive symptoms than a standard smoking cessation protocol. Significantly higher rates of abstinence were maintained through the 6-month follow-up among participants in the integrated, transdiagnostic treatment. To the best of our knowledge, these findings are the first to provide an initial test of the efficacy of a transdiagnostic smoking–anxiety treatment that addresses the unique needs of smokers with HIV. If replicated and extended, this intervention offers a viable treatment option for clinicians who seek to provide personalized, empirically supported care to smokers with HIV.
Supported by grant R34 DA031038-01 award to the first 3 authors by the National Institute on Drug Abuse (NIDA), clinicaltrials.gov Identifier: NCT01393301.

review_title: Interventions for tobacco use cessation in people living with HIV
